tier_2_handbook.definition: Esophageal cancer refers to malignant neoplasms arising from the esophagus, a muscular tube connecting the pharynx to the stomach. Embryologically, the esophagus derives from the foregut, with its upper third originating from the pharyngeal gut and the lower two-thirds from the embryonic esophagus. Anatomically, the esophagus extends from the cricopharyngeus muscle to the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ), which is defined as the point where the tubular esophagus meets the saccular stomach, often marked by the squamocolumnar junction (SCJ) or Z-line. The esophagus is lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, which can undergo metaplasia to columnar epithelium (Barrett's esophagus), a precursor to adenocarcinoma. The esophagogastric junction (EGJ) is the junction between the esophagus and stomach, with tumors involving this area classified based on their epicenter relative to the GEJ. For adenocarcinomas, the Siewert classification categorizes EGJ tumors: Type I (1-5 cm above GEJ), Type II (true cardia, within 1 cm above and 2 cm below GEJ), and Type III (2-5 cm below GEJ, infiltrating upward). Staging utilizes the TNM system, with T categories reflecting depth of invasion from mucosa (T1) to adventitia (T3) and adjacent structures (T4) [10, 73].

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
subtypes.description: The predominant histologic type worldwide, arising from the squamous epithelium. SCC is more common in the upper and middle thirds of the esophagus and is strongly associated with tobacco and alcohol use. It is the most common subtype in Eastern Europe and Asia, forming a 'cancer belt' from northern Iran through Central Asia to northern China.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for less than 30% of esophageal cancers in the United States and Western Europe, but is the majority subtype in high-incidence areas like Eastern Asia.
subtypes.key_associations: Tobacco use, alcohol consumption, nutritional deficiencies (e.g., zinc, selenium), caustic injury, achalasia, tylosis, Fanconi anemia, and Plummer-Vinson syndrome.

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Arises from glandular epithelium, most commonly in the setting of Barrett's esophagus (intestinal metaplasia). Adenocarcinomas are typically located in the distal third of the esophagus and at the EGJ. The incidence has been rising in North America and Western Europe.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for over 70% of esophageal cancers in North America and Western Europe. Approximately 15-30% of esophageal adenocarcinomas are HER2-positive.
subtypes.key_associations: Chronic gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), obesity, Barrett's esophagus, male sex, white race, smoking, family history, and hiatal hernia.

subtypes.name: Rare Histologic Subtypes
subtypes.description: Include adenosquamous carcinoma, small cell carcinoma, sarcoma, lymphoma, and melanoma. These are staged using the TNM system for squamous cell carcinoma.
subtypes.frequency: Collectively account for less than 5% of esophageal malignancies.

subtypes.name: Molecular Subtypes
subtypes.description: Defined by specific biomarkers: HER2 overexpression/amplification, microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or deficient mismatch repair (dMMR), PD-L1 expression (combined positive score [CPS] or tumor area positivity [TAP]), tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H), NTRK gene fusions, RET gene fusions, and BRAF V600E mutations.
subtypes.frequency: HER2 positivity: 15-30% in adenocarcinoma, 5-13% in SCC. MSI-H/dMMR: found in a small subset of esophageal cancers. PD-L1 expression varies.
subtypes.key_associations: These molecular alterations guide targeted therapy selection.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Esophageal carcinogenesis involves a multistep accumulation of genetic and epigenetic alterations. For adenocarcinoma, the sequence typically progresses from normal squamous epithelium to intestinal metaplasia (Barrett's esophagus), low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and invasive cancer. Key molecular events include overexpression of growth factors/receptors (e.g., HER2/ERBB2 amplification, EGFR overexpression), loss of tumor suppressors (e.g., TP53 mutations, CDKN2A loss), and alterations in DNA damage response pathways. Chromosomal abnormalities include gains of chromosomes 8q, 20q, and losses of 4q and 18q. Specific oncogenic drivers include: HER2 (ERBB2) amplification (15-30% of adenocarcinomas), which activates the PI3K-AKT and RAS-MAPK pathways; recurrent mutations in TP53 (>80% of cases), CDKN2A, ARID1A, and SMAD4. For squamous cell carcinoma, tobacco carcinogens (e.g., nitrosamines) cause direct DNA damage, leading to TP53 mutations (often atypical, causing a G:C to T:A transversion), amplification of CCND1 (cyclin D1) on 11q13, and loss of CDKN2A. Tumors with MSI-H/dMMR have defects in DNA mismatch repair genes (e.g., MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), resulting in a high mutational burden and sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors. PD-L1 overexpression is an immune evasion mechanism, where tumor cells express PD-L1 to inhibit T-cell activity via PD-1. NTRK, RET gene fusions, and BRAF V600E mutations are rare but actionable alterations in both histologic subtypes [10, 147, 148, 150, 162-164, 403-406, 431].

epidemiology.incidence: Globally, esophageal cancer is the seventh most commonly diagnosed cancer and the sixth leading cause of cancer-related death, with an estimated 604,000 new cases and 544,000 deaths in 2020 [2, 3]. The global incidence shows a 60-fold variation, with the highest rates in a 'cancer belt' from northern Iran through Central Asia to northern China, where SCC predominates [4, 5]. In the United States, it is the twentieth most commonly diagnosed cancer, with an estimated 21,560 new cases in 2023 [8].
epidemiology.mortality: The 5-year relative survival rate remains low overall. In the United States, the 5-year survival rate is approximately 20% for all stages combined, but varies significantly by stage: localized disease has a 48% survival rate, regional disease 26%, and distant metastatic disease 6% [8].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of SCC has declined in North America and Western Europe due to reduced tobacco and alcohol use. Conversely, the incidence of adenocarcinoma has been rising, likely reflecting increasing rates of obesity, GERD, and Barrett's esophagus [1, 12, 16, 17].
epidemiology.demographics: Esophageal cancer is more common in males than females (approximately 3:1 ratio) for both histologic subtypes. For adenocarcinoma, it is more frequent in white males over age 50. SCC shows racial variations, with higher incidence in African Americans in the U.S. and in certain ethnic groups in high-risk regions globally [1, 6, 7].
epidemiology.geographic_variation: The highest incidence areas for SCC include Eastern Asia, Eastern Europe, and parts of South America. For adenocarcinoma, high rates are seen in North America, Western Europe, and Australia [1, 4, 6].

risk_factors.detail: A major risk factor for esophageal SCC, with a dose-response relationship. Risk for SCC decreases substantially after cessation but remains elevated for years. Smoking is a moderate risk factor for adenocarcinoma.
risk_factors.strength: Major risk factor for SCC; contributes to adenocarcinoma risk.

risk_factors.detail: A significant risk factor for esophageal SCC, especially in combination with smoking. The risk increases with the amount and duration of consumption.
Chronic GERD is a major risk factor for Barrett's esophagus and subsequently adenocarcinoma. The risk increases with severity and duration of symptoms.
risk_factors.strength: Major risk factor for adenocarcinoma via Barrett's esophagus.

risk_factors.detail: The strongest risk factor for esophageal adenocarcinoma. Obesity promotes GERD and has independent carcinogenic effects through chronic inflammation and hormonal factors.

Patients with Barrett's esophagus (intestinal metaplasia) have a 30- to 60-fold increased risk of developing esophageal adenocarcinoma. Risk increases with length of Barrett's segment and presence of dysplasia.
Esophageal cancer is 3 times more common in males than females for both histologic subtypes.
Incidence increases with age, with a median age at diagnosis in the late 60s to 70s.
Adenocarcinoma is more common in white individuals, while SCC shows higher incidence in African Americans in the U.S. and in certain ethnic groups in high-risk regions.

risk_factors.factor: Family history of esophageal cancer or Barrett's esophagus
risk_factors.detail: A family history of Barrett's esophagus or esophageal adenocarcinoma increases risk, suggesting a genetic predisposition. Specific hereditary syndromes include tylosis (RHBDF2 mutations), Bloom syndrome (BLM gene), and Fanconi anemia (FANCA, FANCC genes).
risk_factors.strength: Moderate risk factor; strong in specific syndromes.

risk_factors.detail: A motility disorder causing impaired lower esophageal sphincter relaxation, increasing risk for esophageal SCC due to chronic stasis and irritation.
Ingestion of corrosive agents can cause scarring and strictures, increasing the long-term risk for esophageal SCC.
A triad of iron deficiency anemia, esophageal web, and dysphagia, associated with increased risk for esophageal SCC.
Diets low in fruits and vegetables may increase risk, particularly for SCC. Specific deficiencies (e.g., zinc, selenium, vitamins A, C, E) are implicated in high-incidence regions.
risk_factors.strength: Contributing factor, especially in nutrient-deficient areas.

risk_factors.detail: Consumption of very hot beverages (e.g., mate tea) is associated with increased risk for esophageal SCC, possibly due to thermal injury.

protective_factors: No protective factors are explicitly identified in the NCCN Guidelines for esophageal and EGJ cancers.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Esophageal cancer typically presents with progressive dysphagia, initially to solids then liquids, often accompanied by weight loss and anorexia. Dysphagia occurs due to luminal obstruction from the tumor. Patients may report food sticking, odynophagia (painful swallowing), or a sensation of retrosternal pressure. Weight loss is multifactorial, resulting from decreased oral intake, increased metabolic demands, and malabsorption. Local invasion can cause chest or back pain, cough, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement), or aspiration pneumonia. Advanced disease may present with symptoms of metastasis (e.g., bone pain, jaundice) or complications like esophageal fistula or hemorrhage.

symptoms.name: Dysphagia
symptoms.detail: Progressive difficulty swallowing, typically starting with solids and advancing to liquids.
symptoms.alarm: Progressive dysphagia is a critical alarm feature.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, often >10% of body weight.
symptoms.alarm: Significant weight loss is a concerning symptom.

symptoms.name: Chest/Back pain
symptoms.detail: Retrosternal or epigastric pain, may radiate to the back.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent pain may indicate local invasion.

symptoms.name: Cough/Aspiration
symptoms.detail: Chronic cough, hoarseness, or recurrent pneumonia due to aspiration.

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal bleeding
symptoms.detail: Hematemesis, melena, or iron deficiency anemia from tumor ulceration.
symptoms.alarm: Active bleeding is a critical alarm.

signs.name: Cachexia
signs.detail: Muscle wasting, significant weight loss, and general debility.

signs.name: Lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymph nodes (Virchow's node).

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: Enlarged liver, may suggest metastatic disease.

signs.name: Hoarseness
signs.detail: Due to recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement.
signs.frequency: Less common, but a late sign

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage disease may be asymptomatic or present with vague symptoms like heartburn or indigestion, leading to delayed diagnosis.
Barrett's esophagus, a precursor to adenocarcinoma, can progress silently without alarm symptoms.
Tumors in the proximal esophagus may initially cause only mild dysphagia that is attributed to other causes.

red_flags: Unintentional significant weight loss (>10% body weight)
New-onset hoarseness or recurrent aspiration pneumonia

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for direct visualization, tissue sampling, and histologic diagnosis. The location, length, and extent of tumor involvement must be documented.
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor morphology, depth of invasion (if visible), and confirmation of histology (SCC vs. adenocarcinoma).

diagnostic.rationale: Provides detailed imaging of the esophageal wall layers (T staging) and periesophageal lymph nodes (N staging). Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of suspicious nodes increases accuracy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Depth of tumor invasion (T stage), presence of malignant lymph nodes (N stage).

diagnostic.test: Computed Tomography (CT) of chest and abdomen with IV contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Evaluates for locoregional extent, distant metastases (M staging), and lymph node involvement.
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor size, mediastinal invasion, distant metastases (liver, lungs, peritoneum), enlarged lymph nodes.

diagnostic.rationale: More sensitive than CT for detecting distant metastases and occult regional lymph node disease. Should be performed if no evidence of M1 disease on initial staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Metabolic activity in primary tumor, lymph nodes, and distant sites.

diagnostic.rationale: Indicated if tumor is located at or above the carina to rule out tracheobronchial invasion or synchronous lung primaries.
diagnostic.key_findings: Invasion of the airway or positive bronchial washings.

diagnostic.rationale: Considered in select patients with EGJ adenocarcinoma to detect occult peritoneal metastases or positive peritoneal cytology, which would upstage to M1.

staging.rationale: Gold standard for local T and N staging.
staging.indication: All patients with locoregional disease without evidence of M1.

staging.rationale: Systemic staging to assess for distant metastases.
Detects distant metastatic disease that may change management.
staging.indication: All patients with no evidence of M1 disease on initial workup.

staging.rationale: Provides the most accurate pathologic staging for early-stage (Tis, T1a, superficial T1b) cancers.
staging.indication: For accurate staging of early lesions; may also be therapeutic.

biomarkers.test: Microsatellite Instability (MSI) by PCR/NGS or Mismatch Repair (MMR) by IHC
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors who may benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
biomarkers.when: Universal testing recommended for all newly diagnosed patients with esophageal or EGJ cancers.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, for all newly diagnosed patients.

biomarkers.rationale: PD-L1 expression (using CPS or TAP score) predicts benefit from certain immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, tislelizumab, durvalumab).
biomarkers.when: Universal testing recommended for all newly diagnosed patients.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, for all newly diagnosed patients.

biomarkers.test: Human Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor 2 (HER2/ERBB2) by IHC and ISH/FISH
biomarkers.rationale: Overexpression/amplification identifies patients with HER2-positive disease who benefit from trastuzumab-based therapy.
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented or suspected.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but indicated for advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma.

biomarkers.rationale: Expression identifies patients who may benefit from zolbetuximab.
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented or suspected.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but indicated for advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma.

biomarkers.test: Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) or targeted gene PCR
biomarkers.rationale: Can identify various targetable alterations (e.g., BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, RET fusions, TMB-H).
biomarkers.when: Should be considered, especially if tissue is limited or for identifying rare targetable mutations.

do_not_miss: EGD with biopsy for histologic diagnosis
Complete staging workup (CT, EUS, FDG-PET/CT) in medically fit patients
Universal MSI/MMR and PD-L1 testing at diagnosis
avoid: Omitting EUS for accurate local staging in resectable disease
FDG-PET/CT in patients with known M1 disease (generally not needed for restaging)
Surgical resection without adequate preoperative staging (CT, EUS, FDG-PET/CT)

t_stages.description: Carcinoma in situ; high-grade dysplasia/intraepithelial neoplasia.
Tumor invades the lamina propria, muscularis mucosae, or submucosa.
Tumor invades the lamina propria or muscularis mucosae.
Tumor invades the pleura, pericardium, azygos vein, diaphragm, or peritoneum.
Tumor invades other adjacent structures, such as the aorta, vertebral body, or airway.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Metastasis in 7 or more regional lymph nodes.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade dysplasia/carcinoma in situ, confined to epithelium.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Nearly 100% with appropriate treatment (endoscopic or surgical).

stage_groups.criteria: Varies by histology, but generally includes T1a-T1b, N0, M0 (low-risk)
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to the mucosa or submucosa without nodal involvement.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: >85% for T1a; ~70% for T1b depending on risk factors.

stage_groups.criteria: Varies by histology, includes T2-T3, N0, M0; or T1-T2, N1, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades deeper muscle layers or has limited nodal spread.

stage_groups.criteria: Varies by histology, includes T3-T4a, N1-N2, M0; or T1-T3, N2-N3, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease with significant nodal involvement or invasion into adventitia/adjacent structures (resectable).

stage_groups.criteria: T4b (any N, M0) OR any T, N3, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Unresectable local disease due to invasion of critical structures (T4b) or bulky nodal disease (N3).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy and/or definitive chemoradiation
Palliative; systemic therapy and best supportive care

staging_pearls: The AJCC 8th edition uses separate stage groupings for clinical (cTNM), pathologic (pTNM), and post-neoadjuvant therapy (ypTNM) staging.
For esophageal SCC, tumor location (upper, middle, lower) influences stage groupings.

For adenocarcinoma of the EGJ, the Siewert classification helps guide surgical approach: Type I (epicenter 1-5 cm above EGJ) and II (epicenter 1 cm above to 2 cm below EGJ) are staged as esophageal cancer; Type III (epicenter 2-5 cm below EGJ) is staged as gastric cancer.
Celiac lymph node involvement in EGJ/distal esophageal cancers should be considered for combined modality therapy.
The number of lymph nodes examined is critical: at least 16 nodes should be removed for adequate staging in surgery without neoadjuvant therapy.

management_principles.overview: The management of esophageal and esophagogastric junction (EGJ) cancers requires a multimodal, multidisciplinary approach. Treatment is guided by histologic subtype (squamous cell carcinoma [SCC] vs. adenocarcinoma), clinical stage (TNM classification), anatomic location (cervical, thoracic, or EGJ), performance status (PS), and biomarker status (HER2, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, CLDN18.2). The primary goal is cure for localized disease and prolongation of survival with palliation for advanced disease. The panel strongly recommends multidisciplinary evaluation for all patients with localized disease. Best supportive care is an integral component of treatment at all stages.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with clinically staged I-IVA (except T4b or unresectable N3) disease who are medically fit for potentially curative therapy.
by_intent.approach: Primary treatment options include endoscopic therapy for early-stage (pTis, pT1a, select pT1b) disease; esophagectomy for T1b or greater tumors; and preoperative or definitive chemoradiation, or perioperative chemotherapy (for adenocarcinoma) for locally advanced resectable disease. Treatment decisions are based on precise staging (endoscopic resection, EUS, CT/PET), histologic type, and Siewert classification for EGJ tumors.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with T4b (unresectable due to invasion of heart, great vessels, trachea) or metastatic (M1) disease, or those who decline or are medically unfit for curative-intent therapy.
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy and/or best supportive care, with local therapies (radiation, endoscopic interventions) for symptom control (e.g., dysphagia, obstruction, bleeding). Treatment is guided by PS, biomarker status (MSI-H/dMMR, HER2, PD-L1, NTRK, RET, BRAF), and patient goals.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: A multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach is essential for all patients with localized esophagogastric cancer. The panel recommends an infrastructure that encourages joint treatment decision-making by members of all relevant disciplines, including surgical oncology, medical oncology, gastroenterology, radiation oncology, radiology, pathology, nutrition, social work, and palliative care. MDT meetings should occur frequently (weekly or biweekly) to review patient data collectively, develop individualized treatment plans, and ensure optimal sequencing of therapies. The MDT may consider clinical trials when available and applicable.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a critical determinant of treatment eligibility and intensity. The Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) and Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) PS scales are used. Patients with KPS ≥60% or ECOG PS ≤2 are candidates for systemic therapy with or without local therapies. Patients with KPS <60% or ECOG PS ≥3 should be offered best supportive care and/or palliative radiation; systemic therapy is generally not recommended due to potential lack of benefit and increased toxicity. PS should be regularly reassessed to guide ongoing management.

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Locoregional Disease (Stage I-IVA, except T4b/unresectable N3) - Medically Fit for Surgery

nodes.criteria: pTis (in situ carcinoma) or pT1a (lamina propria/muscularis mucosae invasion).

options.label: Endoscopic resection (ER) ± ablation
options.recommendation: ER (EMR or ESD) ± ablation is the preferred primary treatment for patients with pTis or pT1a disease. Ablation may be needed if there is multifocal high-grade dysplasia/carcinoma in situ. The goal is complete removal/eradication of early-stage disease and pre-neoplastic tissue.

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy is indicated for patients with extensive pTis or pT1a, especially nodular disease not adequately controlled by endoscopic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A. After successful endoscopic therapy, patients undergo endoscopic surveillance per ESOPH-A. After esophagectomy, surveillance per ESOPH-9.

nodes.criteria: pT1b, N0 (submucosal invasion, no lymph node metastasis).

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy (transhiatal, transthoracic, or minimally invasive; gastric reconstruction preferred) is the recommended primary treatment. A feeding jejunostomy may be placed for postoperative nutritional support.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A. Surveillance per ESOPH-9.

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT2,N0 (low-risk lesions: <3 cm, well-differentiated).

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy is the recommended primary treatment for low-risk cT1b-cT2,N0 lesions.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A. Surveillance per ESOPH-9.

nodes.criteria: cT2,N0 (high-risk lesions: LVI, ≥3 cm, poorly differentiated) OR cT1b-cT2,N+ OR cT3-cT4a, Any N.

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Preoperative chemoradiation is the preferred primary treatment. Preferred regimens include paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1). Histologic confirmation of suspected positive nodes is desirable.

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Definitive chemoradiation is appropriate for patients who decline surgery or have cT4b (unresectable) tumors. Preferred regimens are the same as for preoperative chemoradiation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After preoperative chemoradiation, restaging with FDG-PET/CT and EGD/biopsy is performed. Management based on response (see treatment_response_assessment). After definitive chemoradiation, surveillance per ESOPH-9.

nodes.criteria: cT4b (unresectable: invasion of trachea, great vessels, vertebral body, or heart).

options.label: Chemotherapy alone
options.recommendation: Consider chemotherapy alone in the setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart.

options.label: Palliative/endoluminal stenting
options.recommendation: For select patients, consider endoluminal stenting when appropriate for symptom palliation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: These patients are generally managed with palliative intent (ESOPH-10).

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Locoregional Disease (Stage I-IVA, except T4b/unresectable N3) - Nonsurgical Candidates

nodes.criteria: pTis, pT1a (nonsurgical candidate).

options.label: ER ± ablation or ablation alone
options.recommendation: Endoscopic therapies (ER ± ablation, or ablation for pTis) are the recommended primary treatment for nonsurgical candidates with pTis or pT1a disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up with endoscopic surveillance per ESOPH-A.

nodes.criteria: pT1b,N0 (nonsurgical candidate).

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: ER ± ablation may be considered for superficial pT1b disease in nonsurgical candidates.

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Consider definitive chemoradiation for tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poorly differentiated, submucosal invasion ≥200 µm, positive margins, tumor diameter ≥2 cm).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After endoscopic therapy, surveillance per ESOPH-A. After definitive chemoradiation, surveillance per ESOPH-9.

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT4a,N0-N+ or cT4b (unresectable) AND medically able to tolerate chemoradiation.

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Definitive chemoradiation is the recommended primary treatment for nonsurgical candidates able to tolerate chemoradiation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment with FDG-PET/CT, CT, and EGD/biopsy after completion of therapy. Management per ESOPH-5 (for persistent disease) or surveillance (for no evidence of disease).

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT4a,N0-N+ or cT4b (unresectable) AND medically unable to tolerate chemoradiation.

options.label: Palliative radiation therapy
options.recommendation: Palliative radiation therapy for symptom control (e.g., dysphagia, pain, bleeding).

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Palliative/best supportive care, including endoscopic stenting, dilation, feeding tube placement, and management of symptoms.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Ongoing symptom management and supportive care.

management_pathways.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma - Stage IVA (T4b/unresectable N3 only) and IVB (Metastatic) Disease
branching_basis: Biomarker status (MSI/MMR, PD-L1, NTRK, RET, BRAF)

nodes.criteria: Karnofsky PS ≥60% or ECOG PS ≤2.

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is recommended. Preferred first-line regimens include fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + nivolumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + pembrolizumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + tislelizumab-jsgr (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), or fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin (for PD-L1 negative). Oxaliplatin is preferred over cisplatin due to lower toxicity.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment per clinical and radiologic findings. Subsequent therapy based on response, PS, and available options.

nodes.criteria: Karnofsky PS <60% or ECOG PS ≥3.

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Palliative/best supportive care. Systemic therapy is generally not recommended due to poor PS.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Ongoing symptom management and supportive care.

nodes.criteria: MSI-H/dMMR tumors (independent of PD-L1 status).

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab is a preferred option for second-line or subsequent therapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors.

options.label: Dostarlimab-gxly
options.recommendation: Dostarlimab-gxly is a preferred option for second-line or subsequent therapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors.

options.label: Nivolumab and ipilimumab
options.recommendation: Nivolumab and ipilimumab is a preferred option for second-line or subsequent therapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy and PS.

nodes.criteria: TMB-high (≥10 mutations/megabase) tumors.

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab may be used for second-line or subsequent therapy for TMB-high tumors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy and PS.

nodes.criteria: NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion-positive tumors.

options.label: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib
options.recommendation: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib may be used for second-line or subsequent therapy for NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion-positive tumors. Repotrectinib can be used in patients whose disease progressed on prior TRK-targeted therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy and PS.

nodes.criteria: RET gene fusion-positive tumors.

options.label: Selpercatinib
options.recommendation: Selpercatinib may be used for second-line or subsequent therapy for RET gene fusion-positive tumors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy and PS.

nodes.criteria: BRAF V600E-mutated tumors.

options.label: Dabrafenib and trametinib
options.recommendation: Dabrafenib and trametinib may be used for second-line or subsequent therapy for BRAF V600E-mutated tumors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy and PS.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma - Locoregional Disease (Stage I-IVA, except T4b/unresectable N3) - Medically Fit for Surgery

nodes.criteria: pTis, pT1a, superficial pT1b (endoscopic staging).

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: ER (EMR or ESD) ± ablation is the preferred primary treatment for patients with pTis, pT1a, or superficial pT1b (≤500 µm invasion, well/moderate differentiation, no LVI) disease.

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy is indicated for patients with extensive pTis, pT1a, or superficial pT1b, especially nodular disease not adequately controlled by endoscopic therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After endoscopic therapy, surveillance per ESOPH-A. After esophagectomy, surveillance per ESOPH-20.

nodes.criteria: pT1b,N0 (submucosal invasion, no lymph node metastasis).

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy is the recommended primary treatment for pT1b,N0 disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Surveillance per ESOPH-20.

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT2,N0 (low-risk lesions: <3 cm, well-differentiated).

options.label: Perioperative systemic therapy (category 1)
options.recommendation: Perioperative systemic therapy is the preferred primary treatment. Preferred regimen is fluorouracil, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, and docetaxel (FLOT) (category 1).

options.label: Esophagectomy
options.recommendation: Esophagectomy is an alternative primary treatment for low-risk cT1b-cT2,N0 disease.

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Preoperative chemoradiation may be considered for borderline resectable tumors or patients not candidates for FLOT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After perioperative FLOT, postoperative FLOT is continued if tolerated. After preoperative chemoradiation, restaging and consideration for surgery or continued therapy based on response.

nodes.criteria: cT2,N0 (high-risk lesions: LVI, ≥3 cm, poorly differentiated) OR cT1b-cT2,N+ OR cT3-cT4a, Any N.

options.label: Perioperative systemic therapy (category 1)
options.recommendation: Perioperative systemic therapy is the preferred primary treatment for cT1b-cT2,N+ or cT3-cT4a, Any N disease. Preferred regimen is FLOT (category 1).

options.label: Preoperative chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Preoperative chemoradiation is an alternative primary treatment. Preferred regimens are paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1).

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.preference: Only for patients who decline surgery
options.recommendation: Definitive chemoradiation is an option for patients who decline surgery. Preferred regimens are the same as for preoperative chemoradiation.

options.label: Neoadjuvant/Perioperative immunotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors
options.recommendation: For patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors, perioperative immunotherapy should be considered in consultation with a multidisciplinary team. Options include dostarlimab-gxly (neoadjuvant only), nivolumab and ipilimumab followed by nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or tremelimumab and durvalumab (neoadjuvant only).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After perioperative FLOT, postoperative FLOT is continued if tolerated. After preoperative chemoradiation, restaging and consideration for surgery. For MSI-H/dMMR tumors treated with neoadjuvant immunotherapy, surgery remains the standard outside of organ-preservation trials. If undergoing non-operative management, immunotherapy for ≥1 year and intensive surveillance with CT and EGD every 12 weeks for 2 years, then every 6 months until year 5.

nodes.criteria: cT4b (unresectable: invasion of trachea, great vessels, vertebral body, or heart).

options.label: Chemotherapy alone
options.recommendation: Consider chemotherapy alone in the setting of invasion of trachea, great vessels, or heart.

options.label: Palliative/endoluminal stenting
options.recommendation: For select patients, consider endoluminal stenting when appropriate for symptom palliation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: These patients are generally managed with palliative intent (ESOPH-21).

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma - Locoregional Disease (Stage I-IVA, except T4b/unresectable N3) - Nonsurgical Candidates

nodes.criteria: pTis, pT1a (nonsurgical candidate).

options.label: ER ± ablation or ablation alone
options.recommendation: Endoscopic therapies (ER ± ablation, or ablation for pTis) are the recommended primary treatment for nonsurgical candidates with pTis or pT1a disease.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow-up with endoscopic surveillance per ESOPH-A.

nodes.criteria: pT1b,N0 (nonsurgical candidate).

options.label: ER ± ablation
options.recommendation: ER ± ablation may be considered for superficial pT1b disease in nonsurgical candidates.

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Consider definitive chemoradiation for tumors with poor prognostic features (LVI, poorly differentiated, submucosal invasion >500 µm, positive margins, tumor diameter ≥2 cm).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After endoscopic therapy, surveillance per ESOPH-A. After definitive chemoradiation, surveillance per ESOPH-20.

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT4a,N0-N+ or cT4b (unresectable) AND medically able to tolerate chemoradiation.

options.label: Definitive chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Definitive chemoradiation is the recommended primary treatment for nonsurgical candidates able to tolerate chemoradiation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment with FDG-PET/CT, CT, and EGD/biopsy after completion of therapy. Management per ESOPH-15/16 (for persistent disease) or surveillance (for no evidence of disease).

nodes.criteria: cT1b-cT4a,N0-N+ or cT4b (unresectable) AND medically unable to tolerate chemoradiation.

options.label: Palliative radiation therapy
options.recommendation: Palliative radiation therapy for symptom control (e.g., dysphagia, pain, bleeding).

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Palliative/best supportive care, including endoscopic stenting, dilation, feeding tube placement, and management of symptoms.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Ongoing symptom management and supportive care.

management_pathways.title: Adenocarcinoma - Stage IVA (T4b/unresectable N3 only) and IVB (Metastatic) Disease
branching_basis: Biomarker status (HER2, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, CLDN18.2, NTRK, RET, BRAF)

nodes.criteria: Karnofsky PS ≥60% or ECOG PS ≤2.

options.label: First-line systemic therapy (HER2-positive)
options.recommendation: For HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ and ISH/FISH+) tumors, trastuzumab should be added to first-line chemotherapy. Preferred regimens: fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and trastuzumab; or fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1).

options.label: First-line systemic therapy (HER2-negative)
options.recommendation: For HER2-negative tumors, preferred first-line regimens include fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + nivolumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + pembrolizumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + tislelizumab-jsgr (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1), or fluoropyrimidine + oxaliplatin + zolbetuximab-clzb (for CLDN18.2-positive). Oxaliplatin is preferred over cisplatin.

options.label: First-line systemic therapy (MSI-H/dMMR, independent of PD-L1)
options.recommendation: For MSI-H/dMMR tumors, preferred first-line regimens include pembrolizumab, dostarlimab-gxly, or nivolumab and ipilimumab.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment per clinical and radiologic findings. Subsequent therapy based on response, PS, and available options.

nodes.criteria: Karnofsky PS <60% or ECOG PS ≥3.

options.label: Palliative/Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Palliative/best supportive care. Systemic therapy is generally not recommended due to poor PS.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Ongoing symptom management and supportive care.

nodes.criteria: Second-line or subsequent therapy (dependent on prior therapy and PS).

options.label: Preferred second-line regimens
options.recommendation: Preferred second-line regimens include ramucirumab and paclitaxel (category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma), fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (for HER2-positive, category 1), docetaxel (category 1), paclitaxel (category 1), irinotecan (category 1), or trifluridine and tipiracil (for EGJ adenocarcinoma, category 1).

options.label: Useful in certain circumstances (biomarker-selected)
options.recommendation: For specific biomarkers: entrectinib/larotrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive tumors; pembrolizumab or dostarlimab-gxly for MSI-H/dMMR tumors; pembrolizumab for TMB-high tumors; dabrafenib and trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutated tumors; selpercatinib for RET fusion-positive tumors.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on prior therapy, PS, and biomarker status.

nodes.criteria: Patients who have NOT received preoperative chemoradiation: R2 resection (macroscopic residual cancer or M1).

options.label: Palliative Management
options.recommendation: Palliative management for patients not candidates for further therapy.

nodes.criteria: Patients who HAVE received preoperative chemoradiation: R0 resection with ypT0,N0 (pathologic complete response).

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Surveillance per ESOPH-9.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy is recommended after pathologic complete response.

nodes.criteria: Patients who HAVE received preoperative chemoradiation: R0 resection with ypT positive and/or ypN positive (residual disease).

options.label: Nivolumab (category 1)
options.recommendation: Nivolumab is a category 1 recommendation for patients with residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation and R0 resection.

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation until disease progression for patients not candidates for further therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant nivolumab for residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation.

options.recommendation: Observation until disease progression.

options.recommendation: Palliative management for patients with progressive disease.

options.label: Observation

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy is recommended for node-negative disease after R0 resection.

nodes.criteria: Patients who have NOT received preoperative therapy: pT3-pT4a, node-negative, R0 resection.

options.label: Chemotherapy or chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Chemotherapy or chemoradiation is recommended for high-risk node-negative disease. High-risk features include poorly differentiated histology, LVI, perineural invasion, or age <50 years.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy for high-risk node-negative disease.

nodes.criteria: Patients who have NOT received preoperative therapy: Any T, node-positive, R0 resection.

options.label: Chemotherapy or chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Chemotherapy (capecitabine and oxaliplatin or FOLFOX) or chemoradiation (fluoropyrimidine-based) is recommended for node-positive disease. Postoperative chemoradiation is recommended for patients with suboptimal surgery (poor nodal harvest) or understaging at diagnosis.

nodes.criteria: Patients who have NOT received preoperative therapy: R1 resection.

options.label: Chemoradiation (fluoropyrimidine-based)

options.recommendation: Palliative management for patients not candidates for further therapy.

nodes.criteria: Patients who HAVE received preoperative therapy (chemoradiation or chemotherapy): R0 resection with ypT0,N0 (pathologic complete response).

options.label: Systemic therapy (if received perioperatively) or observation
options.recommendation: If perioperative systemic therapy was given, complete the planned postoperative cycles (category 1). If preoperative chemoradiation was given, observation is an alternative.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Complete planned perioperative chemotherapy if given.

nodes.criteria: Patients who HAVE received preoperative therapy: R0 resection with ypT positive and/or ypN positive (residual disease).

options.label: Systemic therapy (if received perioperatively) or nivolumab (if received preoperative chemoradiation)
options.recommendation: If perioperative systemic therapy was given, complete the planned postoperative cycles (category 1). If preoperative chemoradiation was given, nivolumab is a category 1 recommendation for residual disease.

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation until disease progression for patients not candidates for further therapy.

nodes.criteria: Patients who HAVE received preoperative therapy: R1 or R2 resection.

options.label: Chemoradiation (fluoropyrimidine-based) if RT not received preoperatively
options.recommendation: Postoperative chemoradiation is recommended if radiation therapy was not received preoperatively.

options.label: Observation or re-resection
options.recommendation: Observation until disease progression or consideration for re-resection for R1 resection.

options.label: Palliative Management
options.recommendation: Palliative management for R2 resection.

options.label: Imaging and endoscopy

options.label: Dilatation and nutritional support
options.recommendation: Dilatation for anastomotic or chemoradiation-induced stricture. Nutritional assessment and counseling.

nodes.criteria: Symptomatic patients or suspected recurrence.

options.label: Unscheduled evaluation
options.recommendation: Unscheduled evaluation if a patient becomes symptomatic. CT chest/abdomen with contrast. EGD with biopsy if indicated.

items.text: Complete history and physical examination (H&P)
Assess performance status (KPS or ECOG)
Screen for family history of esophageal or EGJ cancers
Assess for distress (use NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List)
Nutritional assessment and counseling
Smoking cessation advice, counseling, and pharmacotherapy as indicated
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with biopsies
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Multiple biopsies (6-8) using standard forceps for histologic and molecular interpretation. Location, length, circumferential involvement, and degree of obstruction should be recorded.

items.text: Endoscopic resection (ER) for accurate staging of early-stage cancers (Tis, T1a, T1b)
items.recommendation: Category 2A. ER is recommended for accurate staging and may be therapeutic.

items.text: Chest/abdomen CT with oral and IV contrast
Pelvis CT with contrast as clinically indicated
FDG-PET/CT evaluation (skull base to mid-thigh) if no evidence of M1 disease
Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), if no evidence of M1 unresectable disease
Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive chemistry profile
Universal testing for microsatellite instability (MSI) by PCR/NGS or mismatch repair (MMR) by IHC
items.recommendation: Category 2A for all newly diagnosed patients

items.text: Universal testing for programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1) by IHC using a companion diagnostic test on FFPE tissue
items.recommendation: Category 2A for all newly diagnosed patients

items.text: HER2 (ERBB2) testing if advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented/suspected
CLDN18.2 testing if advanced/metastatic adenocarcinoma is documented/suspected
Next-generation sequencing (NGS) should be considered via a CLIA-approved laboratory
Bronchoscopy, if tumor is at or above the carina with no evidence of M1 disease
Assign Siewert category for EGJ adenocarcinomas
Multidisciplinary evaluation
Consider enteric feeding tube for preoperative nutritional support

surgery.role: Surgery is a major component of treatment for locoregional esophageal and EGJ cancers. Esophagectomy is the primary treatment for patients with T1b or greater tumors. Preoperative chemoradiation or perioperative chemotherapy is recommended for locally advanced resectable disease to improve survival, reduce locoregional recurrence, and increase R0 resection rates. Surgery is also considered for palliation of symptoms such as dysphagia or fistula.
principles: All patients should be evaluated for physiologic ability to undergo major abdominal and/or thoracic surgery.
Clinical staging with CT, FDG-PET/CT, and EUS should be performed prior to surgery to assess resectability.
Siewert tumor type should be assessed in all patients with adenocarcinomas involving the EGJ. Siewert Type I and II tumors are managed as esophageal cancers; Type III as gastric cancers.
Esophageal resection should be performed in high-volume centers by experienced surgeons.
Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE) approaches should be strongly considered given improved postoperative outcomes compared to open surgery.
Gastric conduit is preferred for reconstruction. Colon or jejunum may be used if the stomach is unavailable.
At least 16 regional lymph nodes should be removed and pathologically examined during resection for curative intent.
Feeding jejunostomy for postoperative nutritional support is generally preferred over gastrostomy, which may compromise gastric conduit vasculature.

procedures.name: Ivor Lewis esophagogastrectomy
procedures.indications: Distal thoracic esophageal or EGJ tumors.
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, pneumonia, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury.
procedures.operative_mortality: Reported mortality rates range from 1-5% in high-volume centers.

procedures.name: McKeown esophagogastrectomy
procedures.indications: Upper, middle, or lower thoracic esophageal tumors. Allows for more extensive esophageal resection.
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak (higher rate than intrathoracic), stricture, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, wound infection.
procedures.operative_mortality: Reported mortality rates range from 1-5% in high-volume centers.

procedures.name: Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE)
procedures.approach: Includes minimally invasive Ivor Lewis (laparoscopy + limited right thoracotomy) and minimally invasive McKeown (right thoracoscopy + limited laparotomy/laparoscopy + cervical anastomosis). Robotic-assisted MIE is an emerging technique.
procedures.indications: Early-stage disease, or as a standard approach for resectable tumors in suitable patients. May be preferred for older patients or those with comorbidities.
procedures.key_complications: Similar to open approaches but with potentially lower rates of pulmonary complications, shorter hospital stay, and faster recovery.
procedures.operative_mortality: Reported mortality rates as low as 1-2% in high-volume centers.

procedures.name: Transhiatal esophagogastrectomy
procedures.indications: Selected distal esophageal or EGJ tumors. Not recommended as a routine approach due to lower lymph node retrieval.
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, incomplete mediastinal dissection.
procedures.operative_mortality: May have lower early morbidity but long-term survival similar to transthoracic approaches.

procedures.name: Left transthoracic or thoracoabdominal esophagogastrectomy
procedures.approach: Contiguous abdominal and left thoracic incision through the eighth intercostal space.
procedures.indications: Selected distal esophageal or EGJ tumors, particularly bulky tumors.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer includes T1-T3 tumors, even with regional nodal metastases (N+), although bulky, multistation nodal involvement is a relative contraindication. T4a tumors (involving pleura, pericardium, diaphragm, or azygos vein) are resectable. Unresectable disease includes cT4b tumors (involving heart, great vessels, trachea, vertebral body, or adjacent organs like liver, pancreas, lung, spleen), most patients with multistation, bulky lymphadenopathy, EGJ tumors with supraclavicular lymph node involvement, and distant metastases (M1). Positive peritoneal cytology (without visible implants) is considered M1 disease.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Patients should be assessed by an esophageal surgeon for physiologic ability to tolerate major surgery. Nutritional assessment and counseling are essential; enteric feeding tube (jejunostomy preferred) should be considered for patients with significant dysphagia/weight loss to support nutrition during induction therapy. Smoking cessation is strongly encouraged. Perioperative cardiopulmonary optimization may be needed.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is an integral part of combined modality treatment for esophageal cancer. It is used in the preoperative (neoadjuvant) setting combined with chemotherapy to improve survival and resectability, in the definitive (non-surgical) setting for patients who are not surgical candidates or decline surgery, and for palliation of symptoms (dysphagia, bleeding, pain). Postoperative RT is considered for patients with positive margins or high-risk features after surgery.
principles: Treatment recommendations should be made by a multidisciplinary team including radiation oncologists.
All available diagnostic studies (CT, EUS, endoscopy reports, FDG-PET/CT) should be reviewed to define target volumes.

CT simulation and conformal treatment planning (3D-CRT or IMRT) are required. Proton beam therapy (PBT) may be considered in clinical settings where reduction in dose to organs at risk is required, ideally within a clinical trial.
Patients should be treated in the supine position for reproducible setup.
Respiratory motion may be significant; 4D-CT planning or other motion management techniques should be used, and margins may be modified.
Target volumes must carefully encompass disease while sparing uninvolved stomach for future reconstruction.
Treatment interruptions or dose reductions for manageable acute toxicities should be avoided; aggressive supportive care is preferred.
Patients should be monitored weekly with vital signs, weight, and blood counts during RT.

dose_frameworks.name: Preoperative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer receiving preoperative chemoradiation.

dose_frameworks.name: Postoperative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients with positive margins or high-risk features after surgery.

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For patients receiving definitive chemoradiation (non-surgical candidates or those declining surgery). Non-surgical candidates should receive the higher dose range (50-50.4 Gy) as lower doses may not be adequate.

approaches.name: Preoperative chemoradiation
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred regimens: paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1). Other recommended: fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1).
approaches.indication: Resectable locoregional esophageal or EGJ cancer. Preferred for adenocarcinoma; also standard for SCC.
approaches.key_trial: CROSS trial (van Hagen et al., NEJM 2012): Preoperative chemoradiation with paclitaxel and carboplatin significantly improved OS (median 49 vs. 24 months; HR 0.657, P=0.003) and DFS compared to surgery alone in patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ cancers.
approaches.toxicities: Esophagitis, neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, weight loss. Postoperative complications (anastomotic leak, pneumonia) are not significantly increased compared to surgery alone.

approaches.name: Definitive chemoradiation
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred regimens: paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1). Other recommended: fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1).
approaches.indication: Unresectable locoregional disease (T4b or bulky lymphadenopathy) or patients who decline surgery.
approaches.key_trial: RTOG 85-01 (Cooper et al., JAMA 1999): Chemoradiation (fluorouracil and cisplatin + 50.4 Gy) significantly improved 5-year OS (20% vs. 0%) and median survival (14 vs. 9 months) compared to RT alone in patients with esophageal cancer.
approaches.toxicities: Esophagitis, neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, weight loss, pneumonitis, stricture.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various short courses (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions, 20 Gy in 5 fractions)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: RT alone is typically used for palliation.
approaches.indication: Symptom control (dysphagia, bleeding, pain) in patients with advanced disease not candidates for curative therapy.
approaches.key_trial: TROG 03.01 (Penniment et al., Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol 2018): Palliative chemoradiation showed minimal improvement in dysphagia relief over RT alone with increased toxicity.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy regimens for advanced esophageal adenocarcinoma, EGJ adenocarcinoma, and gastric adenocarcinoma may be used interchangeably. Regimens for advanced SCC of the esophagus are separately included. Treatment is guided by performance status, comorbidities, toxicity profile, and biomarker status. Two-drug cytotoxic regimens are preferred for advanced disease due to lower toxicity; three-drug regimens are reserved for medically fit patients with excellent PS. Oxaliplatin is preferred over cisplatin due to lower toxicity. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: FLOT significantly improved OS compared to ECF in the FLOT4 trial (median OS 50 vs. 35 months; HR 0.77).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: FLOT + durvalumab (for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or TAP ≥1%; category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma; category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma). Fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing (CPS or TAP score) to guide use of durvalumab. MSI/MMR testing for consideration of immunotherapy.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Neoadjuvant or Perioperative Immunotherapy (for MSI-H/dMMR tumors)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dostarlimab-gxly for neoadjuvant therapy only. Nivolumab and ipilimumab followed by nivolumab. Pembrolizumab. Tremelimumab and durvalumab for neoadjuvant therapy only.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: High response rates and potential for organ preservation in select patients.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR status by PCR/NGS or IHC. Should be considered in consultation with a multidisciplinary team.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1). Fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Evidence from CROSS and other trials supporting efficacy and safety in the preoperative setting.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1). Irinotecan and cisplatin (category 2B). Paclitaxel and fluoropyrimidine (category 2B).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Paclitaxel and carboplatin. Fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Regimens effective in preoperative setting are also preferred for definitive treatment.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1). Irinotecan and cisplatin (category 2B). Paclitaxel and fluoropyrimidine (category 2B).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Postoperative Systemic Therapy (for residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-577 trial showed significantly longer DFS with adjuvant nivolumab (median 22.4 vs. 11 months; HR 0.69).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine and oxaliplatin for patients not receiving nivolumab.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Unresectable Locally Advanced or Metastatic Adenocarcinoma (HER2-positive)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and trastuzumab. Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and trastuzumab (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ToGA trial showed improved OS with trastuzumab added to chemotherapy. KEYNOTE-811 trial showed improved ORR with pembrolizumab added.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HER2 (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ and ISH/FISH+). PD-L1 CPS score to guide pembrolizumab addition.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Unresectable Locally Advanced or Metastatic Adenocarcinoma (HER2-negative)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and zolbetuximab-clzb for CLDN18.2-positive (category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma; category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma). Fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-649, KEYNOTE-590/859, RATIONALE-305, and SPOTLIGHT/GLOW trials demonstrated survival benefits with addition of immunotherapy or zolbetuximab.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine and cisplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS score. CLDN18.2 expression (≥75% tumor cells with moderate to strong membrane staining).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Unresectable Locally Advanced or Metastatic Adenocarcinoma (MSI-H/dMMR, independent of PD-L1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: High response rates to immune checkpoint inhibitors in MSI-H/dMMR tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Chemotherapy regimens with or without immunotherapy.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Unresectable Locally Advanced or Metastatic SCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Oxaliplatin, paclitaxel, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1. Fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate-648, KEYNOTE-590, and RATIONALE-306 trials demonstrated survival benefits.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Cisplatin, paclitaxel, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1. Fluoropyrimidine and cisplatin. Nivolumab and ipilimumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy for Adenocarcinoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ramucirumab and paclitaxel (category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma; category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma). Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for HER2-positive (category 1). Docetaxel (category 1). Paclitaxel (category 1). Irinotecan (category 1). Fluorouracil and irinotecan. Trifluridine and tipiracil for third-line or subsequent therapy for EGJ adenocarcinoma (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: REGARD, RAINBOW, DESTINY-Gastric01, COUGAR-02, and TAGS trials demonstrated survival benefits.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ramucirumab (category 1 for EGJ adenocarcinoma; category 2A for esophageal adenocarcinoma). Irinotecan and cisplatin. Fluorouracil and irinotecan + ramucirumab. Irinotecan and ramucirumab. Docetaxel and irinotecan (category 2B).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HER2 status for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki. MSI/MMR status, TMB, NTRK, RET, BRAF for targeted therapies.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy for SCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Nivolumab (category 1). Pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥10 (category 1). Docetaxel (category 1). Paclitaxel (category 1). Irinotecan (category 1). Tislelizumab-jsgr (category 1). Fluorouracil and irinotecan.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ATTRACTION-3, KEYNOTE-181, and RATIONALE-302 trials demonstrated survival benefits.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Irinotecan and cisplatin. Docetaxel and irinotecan (category 2B).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS score for pembrolizumab. MSI/MMR status, TMB, NTRK, RET, BRAF for targeted therapies.

key_regimens.name: FLOT (Perioperative)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, neuropathy, mucositis.

key_regimens.name: Paclitaxel and Carboplatin (Preoperative/Definitive Chemoradiation)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Esophagitis, neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, peripheral neuropathy.

key_regimens.name: Fluorouracil and Oxaliplatin (FOLFOX) (Preoperative/Definitive Chemoradiation)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, neuropathy, mucositis.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (Adjuvant after preoperative chemoradiation)
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant for residual disease after preoperative chemoradiation
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 4 weeks for up to 1 year
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, rash, musculoskeletal pain, pruritus, diarrhea, immune-mediated adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, nephritis, dermatologic toxicities).

key_regimens.name: Fluoropyrimidine, Oxaliplatin, and Nivolumab (First-Line for Adenocarcinoma)
key_regimens.setting: First-line for unresectable locally advanced or metastatic adenocarcinoma
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, diarrhea, decreased appetite, fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, immune-mediated adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Fluoropyrimidine, Oxaliplatin, and Pembrolizumab (First-Line for Adenocarcinoma/SCC)
key_regimens.setting: First-line for unresectable locally advanced or metastatic adenocarcinoma or SCC
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, diarrhea, decreased appetite, fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, immune-mediated adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Ramucirumab and Paclitaxel (Second-Line for Adenocarcinoma)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line for unresectable locally advanced or metastatic adenocarcinoma
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, peripheral neuropathy, proteinuria.

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (Second-Line for HER2-Positive Adenocarcinoma)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, decreased white blood cell count, nausea, fatigue, interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis (potentially fatal).

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (Second-Line for SCC)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 2 weeks until progression or unacceptable toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, rash, decreased appetite, pruritus, diarrhea, immune-mediated adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (Second-Line for SCC with PD-L1 CPS ≥10)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line for advanced esophageal SCC with PD-L1 CPS ≥10
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks until progression or unacceptable toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, decreased appetite, pruritus, diarrhea, immune-mediated adverse events.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment should be performed ≥5 to 8 weeks after completion of preoperative therapy and prior to surgery. For definitive chemoradiation, response assessment is typically performed 8-12 weeks after completion of therapy.

response_logic.scenario: No evidence of disease after preoperative chemoradiation
response_logic.management: Esophagectomy (preferred) or observation (category 2B). Surveillance per ESOPH-9/20.

response_logic.scenario: Persistent local disease after preoperative chemoradiation
response_logic.management: Esophagectomy (preferred) or palliative management. Repeat multidisciplinary consultation is recommended before proceeding to surgery for post-neoadjuvant T4a and bulky multiple nodal station N3.

response_logic.scenario: Unresectable or metastatic disease after preoperative therapy
No evidence of disease after definitive chemoradiation
response_logic.management: Observation (preferred) or esophagectomy (if surgery was initially planned but deferred). Surveillance per ESOPH-9/20.

response_logic.scenario: Persistent local disease after definitive chemoradiation
New metastatic disease after any therapy

imaging_recommendations: FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or CT chest/abdomen with oral and IV contrast.
Pelvis CT with contrast if clinically indicated.

CT with contrast should be considered if there were findings on pre-treatment CT that merit more accurate anatomic assessment after treatment (e.g., small pulmonary nodules) or if post-treatment PET identified new findings.
EGD with biopsy (optional if surgery is planned after preoperative chemoradiation; recommended after definitive chemoradiation).
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Post-treatment endoscopic biopsy is a poor predictor of pathologic response in patients undergoing chemoradiation therapy.
EUS exams performed after chemotherapy or RT have reduced ability to accurately determine stage of disease.
Consider deferring assessment endoscopy with biopsy to 6 weeks or later after completion of preoperative therapy in patients in whom avoidance of surgery is being considered.
Salvage esophagectomy can be considered for patients with localized, resectable recurrence after definitive chemoradiation if there is no distant recurrence.
